Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/plezy-git.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dwcqc274...

Analyzing plezy-git AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dwcqc274) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines two sources: a Git repository from the project's declared upstream (`https://github.com/edde746/plezy`) and a Flutter SDK archive from Google's official release infrastructure (`storage.googleapis.com/flutter_infra_release`). Both are legitimate and expected sources. The `sha256sums` array correctly uses 'SKIP' for the Git source (standard for VCS sources) and provides a valid checksum for the Flutter tarball.

The `pkgver()` function performs standard version manipulation using `git describe` on the checked-out repository. This runs only after the source is cloned, and the command is innocuous: it doesn't execute arbitrary code or exfiltrate data. There is no top-level command substitution or immediate execution of downloaded code during sourcing. The structure and operations are consistent with normal AUR packaging practices for a Flutter-based application.

While the use of an unpinned Git repository (mutable ref) and downloading the Flutter SDK are notable from a reproducibility and trust standpoint, they are common in -git packages and do not constitute active malicious behavior during the `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` phase.
</details>
<summary>
Sources safe; no execution during fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Sources safe; no execution during fetch[/]

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/plezy-git.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-buoe95np...

Analyzing plezy-git AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-buoe95np) with deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD sources a git repository from the package's own upstream and downloads a Flutter SDK tarball from the official Google storage, both with appropriate checksums (SKIP for VCS, fixed SHA256 for the tarball). The global scope contains only standard variable assignments and no command substitutions or dangerous operations. The `pkgver()` function runs `git describe` and `sed`, which is normal for VCS packages and does not execute untrusted code or make network requests. Since `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` prevent `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` from executing, no potentially suspicious code in those functions can run during this phase. There is no evidence of malicious behavior such as obfuscated commands, unexpected network targets, or data exfiltration.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: standard source fetching and version detection, no malicious code run.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: standard source fetching and version detection, no malicious code run.[/]

Final Status: SKIPPED


No issues found.


Audit Skips:

[SKIPPED] makepkg --nobuild failed, unable to download sources.

data.reasoning: Both models reviewed only the PKGBUILD and judged it SAFE before makepkg --nobuild failed to download sources. Because source download failed, neither model could inspect the actual source tree (prepare/build/package execution paths, any .install hooks, or upstream build scripts). The PKGBUILD itself appears benign: standard VCS git source from declared upstream (github.com/edde746/plezy), a Flutter SDK tarball from official Google storage with a pinned SHA256, and a normal pkgver() using git describe. However, the build/package functions and the upstream repo content were never reviewed, so a full safety verdict cannot be reached. Verdict is inconclusive rather than safe.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no substantive disagreement. Both models reached SAFE on the PKGBUILD-only initial audit and then SKIPPED because source download failed. The trigger ('shallow, reviewed 0 files') reflects that no source files beyond the PKGBUILD were examined, not a conflict between models.
coverage_issues: Source download via makepkg --nobuild failed for both models, so prepare(), build(), and package() function logic was never validated against actual source content.
No .install hook (if present) or upstream build scripts were reviewed.
The Flutter SDK download succeeded checksum verification only in theory; the failure to fetch sources means even the tarball acquisition path was not exercised end-to-end.
false_negatives: Potential risks inside prepare()/build()/package() were not evaluated — Flutter builds commonly run 'flutter pub get' which fetches and executes arbitrary dependency code at build time; this network/code-execution surface was unreviewed.
Any .install scriptlet behavior was not assessed.
re_audit_focus: Resolve the source download failure (possibly network/timeout or large Flutter SDK tarball) and re-run with sources present.
Review prepare/build/package functions, especially any flutter pub get / dart commands that pull dependencies during build.
Check for and review any .install hook.
Inspect upstream plezy build scripts referenced by build().
learnings: A SAFE verdict on the PKGBUILD-only initial pass should never be promoted to a package-level SAFE when source download fails — the correct terminal state is inconclusive/skipped, which the tool correctly did, but downstream consumers should treat 'skipped' as 'not cleared'.

makepkg --nobuild failures (often due to large downloads like the Flutter SDK or transient network issues) are a recurring cause of zero-file reviews; the tool should support retry/extended timeouts or a fallback to clone the upstream git source directly for static review of build scripts.

Flutter/Dart packages have inherent build-time dependency-fetch-and-execute behavior; the audit prompt should flag this class explicitly so reviewers know build() is a meaningful trust surface even when the PKGBUILD looks clean.
When source fetch fails, the tool could still statically clone the declared upstream repo to review build logic, rather than reviewing 0 files.
